steps.title: Recognize the parenthetical use of “however”
steps.content:
The sentence contains a main clause (“Relatively few of these particles are likely to reach Earth's surface”) followed by an explanatory reason clause (“since the meteoroids' compression of the air surrounding them causes the vast majority to burn up in the mesosphere”), with the transition word “however” bridging them.

The question tests how to punctuate “however” when it functions within a complex sentence rather than introducing a new sentence or independent clause.

steps.title: Apply the rule for parenthetical transition words
steps.content:
When a transition word like “however” appears within a sentence as a parenthetical element connecting a main clause to a subordinate clause, it must be surrounded by commas.

This punctuation marks “however” as a transition while keeping the main clause and its explanation tightly connected.

Choice B — “surface, however,” — correctly places commas on both sides of “however,” keeping the main clause and its subordinate 'since' clause tightly connected in one sentence.

  • Choice A — 'surface; however.': uses a semicolon then a period, which strands the 'since' clause as a fragment after the period. ✗
  • Choice C — 'surface, however;': places a semicolon after 'however,' but a semicolon must be followed by an independent clause — the 'since...' clause is subordinate, not independent. ✗
  • Choice D — 'surface. However,': creates a sentence boundary before 'However,' leaving the 'since' clause stranded as a fragment without a main clause. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
